(Multivariate) Gaussian Distribution

Introduction

The Gaussian distribution, also known as the normal distribution, is the most extensively utilized probability distribution for real-valued random variables . In this article, we will delve into the definition of the (multivariate) Gaussian distribution and elucidate why it assumes its particular form through the principle of maximum entropy. Additionally, we will derive the normalization constant in the Gaussian probability density function (PDF).

Univariate Gaussian distribution

The PDF of the Gaussian is given by:

where is the normalization constant that is needed to ensure that the density integrates to 1.

Standard Normal

It is common in the literature to denote standard normal r.v. as instead of :

where is a normalizing constant.

Normalizing constant

In order for one function to be a valid PDF, it has to integrate 1. Concretely, in the case of the Standard Normal PDF, we have to find a constant such that:

And so we need to find the value of by calculating . We start with integral:

However, the problem is that there exist no closed-form solution to this integral. In fact, is an example of what is known as nonelementary integral.

So we need to come up with another clever way to deal with this integral instead of solving it directly. We can start by writing it twice:

For the sake of convenience, we can rewrite (1) as follows:

Now comes the "pulling the rabbit out of the hat" moment, where we'll transform (2) into Polar ordinate instead of Cartesian coordinate.

This is known as Change of variables (please refer to Harvard Stat110's Math Handout as a quick reference). We can thus write the following:

Let , therefore , we can rewrite (3) as:

Recall that we write the term twice (as in (1)), therefore .

Remark.

I've known the Gaussian distribution for a long time because of its popularity but never go as deep as to wondering why we need the term . However, once I do, it always fascinates me every time I go through this derivation.

You can apply the exact same procedure for the Normal distribution (instead of the Standard Normal distribution) to see why . In fact, what we've just derived is the special case when .

Gaussian distribution derivation from Principle of Maximum entropy

From (5) and (6), we obtain that and . Plug in everything together, we have that:

which is exactly the form of the PDF of the Gaussian distribution.

Multivariate Gaussian distribution

Definition

Similar to the univariate Gaussian distribution, the Multivariate Gaussian distribution also has two parameters, the vector mean and the covariance matrix . The mean is a vector that describes the position of the distribution, and the covariance matrix is a symmetric positive definite matrix that describes the shape of the distribution.

Definition (The Multivariate Gaussian distribution).

A vector-valued random variable is said to have a multivariate normal (or Gaussian) distribution with mean and covariance matrix if its probability density function is given by:

We write this as .

Types of Covariance Matrix

There are three forms of the covariance matrix in multivariate normals, including spherical, diagonal, and full covariances. For the bivariate (two dimensional) case, these are:
